I just moved the calculation of `100 / all_delta` that was inside the loop before to outside of the loop. So `(100 * field_delta) / all_delta` becomes `field_delta * scale` where `scale` is "pre-calculated". The `max` bit is to avoid ZeroDivisionError in the case of `all_delta=0`. In this case `field_delta` will also be 0 so the percentages will zero out anyway. There are two issues here:

1. Unicode literal strings which are fixed with the `from __future__ import unicode_literals`.
2. Type casting of strings as unicode in the code. Python3 has no `unicode` keyword so we can't do `unicode(some_string)`. The solution for this in Six Library is:

```
six.text_type(some_string)
```

Which resolves to `str` in Python3 (which is unicode) and `unicode` in Python2.

There were some references in this code which weren't apparent to me how to fix this way, however.

For example,

```
    'string': {
        "native_type": "unicode",
        "base_type": True,
        "quote_arg": True,
        "pytype": unicode
    },
```

You have this `native_type` with a name unicode as a string. So I just did a simpler solution and added the name `unicode` into Python3.

Open to the other fix, however, as it is a better way to do it (i.e. to typecast using the Six library and have no direct unicode references in the code).

If I commented out `visibility_timeout` from default_airflow.cfg, then `broker_transport_options` would still never be None...it would just be an empty dictionary.

>The reason to check for v4 and prefer it over v2 is to correctly handle the case of one of the few remaining v2 services adding support for v4.  At that point, it is unlikely the service would stop supporting v2, so we'd need to account for the case of both v2 and v4 as available signature versions.

I'm comparing how we've rolled out v4 to services so far.  With the exception of S3 (which has its own special signature version anyways) the `v2->v4` upgrade has been completely transparent.  We just update the signatureVersion to say `v4` in the service model.  I picked a handful of services and flipped them back to v2, and they all still work (EC2, ELB, IAM, etc.).  I suspect every service that once supported v2 still supports v2.  Yet we don't call them out here in this endpoints file.  And I don't think we need to.  If the last remaining v2 service, importexport, flips to v4, I imagine we'd just update this file to say that we should start using `v4` for importexport wouldn't we?  There's no need to specify both signature versions in this file.

I don't like that you are mutating the `StringIO` object while iterating over it.
When hitting this code, the html data will be parsed later by lxml, resulting in an lxml document which is kept completely in RAM. Since this will happen eventually we don't have to take care of not putting everything into RAM here.

Therefore I suggest to read the complete string into RAM, replace it, then recreate the StringIO. That should be better readable, simpler, less error prone but it may be consuming more RAM, but we don't care.

For example:
```python
elif isinstance(html, StringIO):
    position = html.tell()
    raw_html = html.getvalue()
    if 'xmlns:d="DAV:"' in raw_html:
        raw_html = raw_html.strip().replace('<D:href>', '<d:href>').replace('</D:href>', '</d:href>')
        html = String(raw_html)
        html.seek(position)
    return html
```

``verify_cache`` called on *connection* (see ``notifyConnected``) when the server tid is > cache tid and when "quick" verification isn't possible because we've been disconnected for too long.

Invalidations are queued while this ``verify_cache`` runs, however, the queueing stops just before the check above.  The logic is torturous in many ways, in part because of ZEO4's use of threads for managing connections, and because the logic is spread over too many methods. ZEO5 will simplify this quite a bit, including getting rid of full cache verification. :)

The race here is that ``server.endZeoVerify`` very indirectly calls ``endVerify``, which calls ``finish_verification``, which stops invalidation queueing before the ``server_tid > self._cache.getLastTid()`` check is done.  Bonus: the indirect call happens in a different client thread!

In my mind, a true semantic error equates to a guaranteed programming error and should always be part of compile. It's true that a typecheck pass could reject a program that works, but then either the types signatures specified incorrectly or the values passed or returned were luckily shaped correctly. A linter, on the other hand, generally includes checks that only enforce opinion - import sorting, line lengths, etc. It's true that some linters include semantic checks making those tools tricky to assign a goal, but for mypy, which is narrowly a type-checker, it seems to me the compile goal is the clear home. I think its probably fairly universal to associate "compile" with typechecking and generation of object code of whch mypy does the former.

>  --permissions [Required]: Permissions of shared access policy.  Allowed values: 
> "RegistryRead, DeviceConnect", 
> "RegistryRead, RegistryWrite",
> "RegistryRead, RegistryWrite, DeviceConnect",
> "RegistryRead, RegistryWrite, ServiceConnect",
> "RegistryRead, RegistryWrite, ServiceConnect, DeviceConnect",
> "RegistryRead, ServiceConnect",
> "RegistryRead, ServiceConnect, DeviceConnect",
> "RegistryWrite, DeviceConnect",
> "RegistryWrite, ServiceConnect",
> "RegistryWrite, ServiceConnect, DeviceConnect",
> "ServiceConnect, DeviceConnect", 
> DeviceConnect,
> RegistryRead,
> RegistryWrite,
> ServiceConnect.

From the CLI perspective, the resulting "help" value (which I cleaned up for clarity) is basically unusable an results in an experience that diverges wildly from what is done elsewhere.

`... --permissions "RegistryRead, RegistryWrite"`

We don't accept comma-separated lists anywhere in the CLI, and while this isn't technically that (it's just a string containing spaces and commas) it's close enough that it will confuse users, who will be accustomed to using space-separated lists.

This enum essentially contains all possible combinations of these four permissions. I would argue that the underlying SDK enum should contain only the four basic values and a permissions object would track which permissions are set, rather than having an enum entry for every valid combination (especially since all combinations are valid).

Then the help text is useful:
>  --permissions [Required]: Space-separated list of permissions for the shared access policy.  Allowed values: 
>                DeviceConnect, RegistryRead, RegistryWrite, ServiceConnect.

And the usage is consistent with the rest of the CLI:
`... --permissions RegistryRead RegistryWrite ServiceConnect` 

Another acceptable pattern used elsewhere in the CLI would be to associate each permission with a single character and then parse the string (storage does this in places):

For example, assume the following mapping:
> DeviceConnect = d
> RegistryRead = r
> RegistryWrite = w
> ServiceConnect = s

Then the usage would look like:
`... --permissions srw` 

I would recommend the first approach (space-separated list of simple enum values) as it is clearer.

To correctly setup Taupage for AWS accounts, we either share the image during build time (for existing accounts), or use sevenseconds on new accounts which deals with it.
Unfortunately, the logic it uses doesn't really scale. It looks for one existing image (`Taupage-AMI-…`) and only if that isn't present shares every image. Moreover, there's no explicit step for this, it's just a side effect of bastion host/VPC initalization. This means that 1) it's impossible to automatically share staging AMIs and 2) if there's any issues the account might be stuck in a weird state.
I've changed the logic to be a lot more straightforward: we define explicit channels in the configuration, fetch the latest AMIs for each channel and then share the AMIs with the accounts if they are not shared. The latest AMI fetch is also done once to avoid bumping into rate limits, since I don't think overriding the Taupage configuration for an account is something that we ever needed, and there's already some parts of sevenseconds that are done globally.

@bhs @palazzem Upon further research while preparing test cases and test scenarios, I came upon a few things to add here:

When it comes to the `to_restore` part, the only place where I can actually see that happening is when using a thread pool (through `concurrent.futures`), as at least both Gevent/asyncio keep their context-locals new for each new task, no no need to restore anything. 

And for the mentioned thread pool, observe threads *do not* get suspended/reactivated (as I suspect happens for Java+Futures+ExecutorService or C#+async). So in theory you would always get a thread that *doesn't need* to keep its previous state (`Continuation` objects would still be needed, though, of course).

That being said:

1) Most of the time the user wouldn't simply need to do a capture/activate cycle, for both the async frameworks and the multi threaded approaches. And when the user needs it, `Continuation` can be available, and any slightly usability issue around it is probably fine at that point. 
2) If we were to provide an `ActiveSpan` just as Java does (say, for sanity purposes on restoring *any* previous data), how much would it actually impact the most common scenarios? Since `ActiveSpan` is basically a proxy, and the main difference is the `finish` vs `deactivate` methods (which are meant to be used through a context manager) is not such a big burden on the API IHMO.

The main reason it's like it is was to avoid any stl header addition here.

The order of bases needs to be *consistent* (since it determines where things are stored in the `values_and_holders` data), but beyond that the specific ordering is not important.

`std::sort` and `std::unique` both add a dependency on `<algorithm>`, which core pybind doesn't have.  I think that, actually, a plain `std::set` would accomplish this even better (i.e. changing `all` to be an `std::set` instead of a vector), but that would also add a stl dependency (on `<set>`).  I'm fine with adding it (in fact, I'd prefer it, though it's not a huge concern), but I know @wjakob has had some reluctance to adding stl headers before, hence my avoiding it in the PR.
